sentences.text: Well, the thing is that I don't.
I remember seeing him, but I-- I think after the Plaszow experience that the-- the last Plaszow experience, I must have somehow pushed a button and said, you know, I can get scared just this far.
And now, I must simply stop thinking.
Because otherwise, something terrible will happen.
And somehow, I must have managed.
I mean, the-- I know they have been there.
I know I remember-- well, I remember one thing that happened.
A trainload of Jews sent-- were being sent to another camp from somewhere.
And when the trainload came to Brunnlitz, about 80% of the people were frozen dead.
They couldn't-- you couldn't even open the wagons.
They were all frozen up.
And I think that's one thing-- that's one thing where you talked about Emilia-- Emily-- Schindler's wife, where she intervened.
She made them open the wagons and take whatever people alive were still there, and bring them to the camp and feed them, and-- it's one-- one of those incidents that I do remember.
Because it caused a stir, of course-- but not-- not very much about seeing him or her.
They were like in another world.
All the Germans were.
I mean there were we-- the sort of the-- the sheep-- the sheep for slaughter.
And there-- they-- they were they.
The SS were the slaughterers and the-- and Schindler, and his wife, and a few others who were trying to prevent them-- and nobody knew how long they could.
Just about.
No.
No, not at all.
No.
I don't think you do at that age.
It's-- it gets too complicated.
I mean, things were complicated enough-- [LAUGHS] as it was.
As I said, it was-- I think it was mostly-- the five years of war, or the last three years, at least, from the time we came into the first camp, from the time of Bochnia, where you simply existed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think you even hoped for the best.
You simply survived every day, and there was a next day.
And you never knew what was going to happen.
And you were-- I do remember, we were-- we were practically always scared.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, fear was something that-- that was so much part of you that I think that if it disappeared, you'd be terribly surprised, because-- because it was-- it was an intrinsic part of you.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think I-- I think when the war-- I think that it took me time after the war not to be afraid, even though the war was over.

[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But I don't really-- I don't even remember many incidents, or any incidents, except for the train thing, and-- oh, silly things like-- [LAUGHS] when the soup used to come in the evening for us to eat, everybody fishing for a-- for a potato or a potato peel-- something that wasn't water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then sometimes, I assume Oskar would do that-- getting eggs all of a sudden.

And I remember the women beating the eggs up, making a sort of mousse-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --sort of little things-- memories that-- that-- that-- that-- that appear-- from-- so-- from nowhere, but nothing continuous.
[SIGHS] Well, only when the Germans left, because I remember that a group of Czechs came into the camp and told the grown-ups, of course, that there were some German families living in Brunnlitz.
And they went on a sort of raid-- not to do anything-- just to rob them-- that just to get food from them and-- because the Germans left a little bit before the Russians came.
And I remember someone telling me that the Czechs thought that we should all sort of go out and kill them.
I don't know whether they did or not.
But that's another sort of-- one of those memories that-- that appears.
I assume that if I sat down and really thought about it a few days, I'd remember it more, or not.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think I was sick at the time.
There was a little-- there was a little hospital there.
And I think I was in the hospital.
I was sick practically throughout the war.
I know that's another thing.
I had typhoid fever, I had an inflammation of the--
--of the joints.
I had scarlet fever-- all the fevers-- [LAUGHS] that-- that you could possibly have.
I had scarlet fever in Plaszow-- no, before that-- in another camp.
And I don't remember the name of that one.
There were camps I was in that I don't even remember the name of.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I think he left after the SS went and just before the Russians came.
No.
I just know that somebody came into the barracks and said, Schindler has left, and the Russians are coming.
And everybody's singing and being very happy, and the war is over.
But-- but you know, it was so hard to believe.
It's been so long.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yes, my mother was-- was in Brunnlitz right to the end with me.
[SIGHS] She must have been there, too, but I don't remember.
Because she came to work with us to Brunnlitz, and nobody was taken away from there.
No, we only knew afterwards when we-- when he joined us in Poland-- that he had been in Gross-Rosen.
Yes.
Well, the Russians-- the Russians asked all of us, I think-- the grownups, of course-- where we were originally from-- what city, et cetera.
And then they put us in trains similar to the-- also cattle trains, but-- but with-- with seats, and beds, and stuff, and food, and sent us right back.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know that.
I-- I can't remember feeling anything in particular.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I think, in a way-- now there comes something I can't talk about.
I think in a way, I stopped feeling, and I never have.
I mean-- sometimes, during the time, I must have stopped feeling anything.
And I don't think I ever felt-- I don't think I ever managed to really-- to feel again.
[CRYING] This is a stupid thing to cry about.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]
Well, he found us, because we went to our house.
Yes, and I don't remember whether to our apartment really, because I think somebody lived there.
Right.
Right.
I don't think it was our apartment.
I think it was in our house-- one of our houses.
But I know that we were there with-- with other people.
But I don't know what other people.
And then my father appeared.
And, of course, then people started searching.
Is so and so alive?
After all, my mother had a very big family.
The only one that was-- that was alive was-- because my mother had half brothers-- brothers and sisters, and half brothers and half sisters.
Because my grandmother-- my-- my grandfather-- I've never known him, but I understand that he was the only Jewish senator ever to be in Poland.
I mean the Jews weren't very liked in Poland.
He was very wealthy and very powerful.
Konigsberg.
That is what my mother told me.
That is not first-hand.
He died long before I was born.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But he was-- everybody knew him-- that I do know.

And well, there we were, trying to find someone, and the only person we found was one of my mother's-- my mother's oldest half-- half sister, [NON-ENGLISH], who also lost all her-- for her-- her two daughters, and her son, and her husband.

And-- and the others, well, we knew that one wasn't-- my mother's younger brother-- youngest brother, who was an engineer, died in the-- in the ghetto in Poland-- but not in Warsaw-- but not in the ghetto-- in the uprising.
He worked in a factory.
And one of the stones broke and cut his face.
And he died from the infection during the war.
So we knew about him.
But all the sisters and all that-- we heard that some of them were on those ships that the Germans said they were sending to Israel and then sank.
But nobody has ever appeared afterwards, so I assume that they all died.
Some were on what we called Polish papers.
Because my mother heard-- had sisters-in-law-- two sisters-in-law and one sister who were blond, and blue-eyed, and very Polish looking.
And they may have passed as-- but obviously they didn't, because they never appeared again.
Trying to get out of Poland-- [LAUGHS]
Well, my father and my mother stayed behind for six month, because we had properties in Poland.
There's something I'm trying-- I'm trying to recuperate my two buildings, which now belong to me.
I don't know whether I ever will.
It's sort of a dream to have some money sometimes and be able to buy my own apartment.
My mother stayed behind to try to sell them.
And of course, she couldn't at that time.
And we, father and I, escaped through Czechoslovakia-- again hiding in-- in trucks filled with vegetables and stuff.
And well, we made it to Munchen-- to Munich.
And my mother came six months later.
And then, well, that's another part of the story.
That's-- that's really after the war.
Well, a cousin of my mother's, Alec Madanes, he lived through the war in England.
He married a Scottish woman.
And he was, at the time, the press attache at the British embassy in Paris.
He came to Germany to see whether any of his family had survived-- and to Munich-- and he found us.
And he practically immediately removed me from there and took me to England, and put me in a school. 'Cause I had never been to a school.
Well, it wasn't too bad, because that particular school, the director-- it was a Dr.
Lyon.
She was Jewish.
And while the school was a non-denominational, there were five or six survivors as students there.
And then, quite honestly, I wasn't-- I was in no position to start making friends and things like that, because I-- I had to learn English.
And then I had to learn how to write, how-- I studied practically the whole time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But it was nice.
And I think it was then that I decided what I had to forget if I wanted to sort of go on and do something with my life.
And I wasn't that old.
I was only 12.
Yes
Yes.
Till '52.
Well, I did per-- I did elementary, secondary, and I got my Bachelor of Arts in those 7 years.
So I did have to do quite a bit of studying.
Oh, that was a-- that was a boarding school.
I stayed at school.
Sure.
No.
No.
They-- they've-- they'd in-- they'd left for Argentina in early '47.
But my-- but Alec, my-- my mother's cousin, I generally spent my holidays with them-- wherever that he was stationed.
And sometimes I was invited to spend the holidays with one of my friends at the school who lived around Haslemere or-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, I went to live in London.
I wanted to stay in England, but, of course, my parents didn't want me to stay there.
And it was very difficult to get a working permit.
Ah, let me see.
That was '52?
19, yes-- not yet-- 18.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] '51, because I finished school in '50.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well, so I had come to Argentina, which I didn't like at all.
And I only stayed for a very short time.
I have stayed, I think for seven months or eight months, and worked as a secretary, and got some money together, and decided I was leaving.
And that's when I went to Israel.
That was '52.
I must have gone in '53 to Israel-- not directly.
I went to Europe and then sort of via Greece and Cyprus-- by ship, of course-- went to Israel and met my aunt.
My grandmother had died already.

My-- she went to-- she-- she went right away to-- right after the war started, she went to-- to Israel to join my aunt, [NON-ENGLISH] who lived-- you see, [NON-ENGLISH]'s son had emigrated to Israel before the war.
And he married an American.
And they lived there.
He brought his mother over once he found her, once they got in touch.
And my grandmother went over there with her.
But when I got there, she had died already.
And well, it was very nice.
I stayed with my aunt, and I worked for the-- whenever I say USOM, nobody-- nobody knows anymore what it is, because it doesn't exist.
It's what the-- I guess, what the United Nations Special Fund does now-- that kind of thing-- technical assistance, et cetera.
So I worked there for a year.
And then my father had a stroke.
Oh, he had several of them before he died.
And my mother asked me to come back.
I went back.
I still didn't like it.
So I stayed for a year and I left again.
[LAUGHS] And I came to the States.
Well, I came to visit my aunt in New-- my aunt-- I call them aunts.
They aren't really.
They're sort of adopted-- adoptive aunts from wartime, et cetera.
And I met some friends of mine who worked with the OAS in Washington, and they offered me a job, the OAS, that is.
So I worked from '58 to '60-- '58 to end of-- end of '59 in Washington.
And then I was offered a job at the UN in New York.
And I stayed there till '64.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then I went-- then I was offered a contract in Senegal, in Dakar.
My daughter was already born by then.
My getting married and all that?
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --my daughter was born in '62.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I had already separated from my first-- from her father-- from my first husband.
That's another-- I think, might be another result of the-- result of the war.
I can't live with people.
I can't live with anyone.
I-- I can't.
I can't even live with my daughter.
[LAUGHS] I had to all those years, but-- but it's difficult for me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was just about to be-- to be married to my second husband, who adopted her.
My second husband?
Gordon James Balfour.
Noel Jacqueline Balfour.
Yes, she was born in New York.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I sent her-- as I said-- I sent her in '64 to Argentina with my mother, because I didn't know how things were in Dakar.

I didn't know whether there were any doctors, or any, you know-- as a matter of fact, I might have spared myself the trouble, because there were very good doctors, and very good hospitals, and very good houses, et cetera.
It's quite civilized.
I stayed there.
I finally didn't-- ended up working-- instead of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs, which-- which-- originally contracted me-- they contracted me.
I finally ended up having a radio program in three languages-- a news program for-- for Europe.
So there I stayed until '65.
And when my mother absolutely refused to send my daughter to Senegal, because she said, no, I'm not going to send her into the darkness of Africa.
I didn't know what could happen to her.
So I went back to Argentina, and that's where I stayed since.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And your daughter has since immigrated to the United States-- has moved here?
Yes, she decided 2 and 1/2 years ago that she was American, and that she didn't care very much for living in Argentina.
Well, she never did.
She always-- she's lived for two years-- two years in Spain, and she's been in Japan, and-- she's as much of a wanderer as I am.
I've always worked as a translator.
I've worked as a freelance.
And for the past four years, I've been looking at the Ministry of Justice as a translator.
Yes.
Yes.
My father died in '71, my mother in '73.
They were very-- they were quite young.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, that's very difficult-- my message to the world.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I think, really, that if grownups want to fight wars, they're welcome to it.
They're grown up.

But they should really leave-- leave the children out of it-- just to give them a chance to have some kind of a normal life afterwards, which I don't think any of us have ever-- could ever manage to have after that.
We-- we tried.
But there's been-- well, a lot of feelings which have been sort of castrated-- when we're too small-- and which we could never recuperate.
No.
No.
No, I thought it would be much too upsetting.
